description: Chunking strategy bakeoff at eval_k=10. Dense-only retrieval (FAISS IndexFlatIP over normalized MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings). No BM25, no RRF fusion, no reranker. These are the corrected numbers after the chunk-vs-doc deduplication fix in commit 446662a — the original notebook run inflated Recall and nDCG by counting multiple chunks of the same relevant doc as separate hits. Phase 2 commits will be measured against this baseline; eval_k=10 is preserved across phases for direct comparability.

corpus.name: huggingface/transformers docs (en)
corpus.notes: Corpus SHA wasn't recorded at fetch time. Subsequent Phase 2 runs should re-fetch and pin the SHA.

eval.dedup: deduplicate_preserving_order at chunk-doc boundary; over-retrieve top_k*4 chunks then dedupe to ~top_k unique doc_ids before computing metrics

headline_findings: Recursive wins on rank-sensitive metrics (MRR, P@1, nDCG@10, Recall@10).

Header is more competitive than the original (inflated) numbers suggested — best on hit_rate_top_5, beats fixed on every metric, gap to recursive on nDCG@10 is ~9% relative (was ~18% before metrics fix).
Fixed performs worst as expected — cutting mid-paragraph damages semantic coherence.

Open question for Phase 2: does the cross-encoder reranker close the recursive-vs-header gap, or even flip the order? Header chunks are larger, which hurts a bi-encoder's single-vector compression but should not penalize a cross-encoder.
